coredata.dc:title: Chiral carbon dots-based nanosensors for Sn(II) detection and lysine enantiomers recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Pengli
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Chirality has a great influence on chemical, biological, and material science. Chiral recognition is fundamental and crucial in chemistry, biology and pharmaceutics. Compared to the extensive attention on achiral carbon dots (CDs), quite a few studies on chiral CDs have been carried out, among which the effects of chiral CDs on recognizing enantiomers have rarely been explored yet. Herein, chiral CDs are utilized as a simple and efficient dual-sensor for Sn2+ detection and Lysine enantiomers recognition for the first time, with the detection limit of 0.057 μM and 3.44 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the strengths of robust stability, high sensitivity and good cytocompatibility enable CDs to successfully monitor the fluctuations of Sn2+ and l-Lys in live cells by virtue of confocal laser scanning microscope. The work highlights the potential of chiral CDs for enantiomeric recognition and ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Nanosensor
Sn2+
Lysine
Enantiomeric recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enantioselective optical sensing performance of CD–Sn

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for fabricating chiral CDs-based nanoprobes for assaying Sn2+ and l-Lys in On-Off-On mode.

(a) Absorption spectra of CDs, Sn2+ and CD–Sn. (b) The emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (c) TEM of CDs, the inset is size distribution of CDs. (d) XPS full spectrum of CDs. High resolution XPS of (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s and (g) N 1s. (h) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (i) CD spectra of CDs and CD–Sn. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence intensity variation of CDs under the conditions of: (a) NaCl, (b) illumination, (c) temperature, (d) pH (λex = 340 nm).

(a) The relative fluorescence intensity of CDs induced by metal ions (10 mM). (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of Sn2+ (0–3.2 mM). (c) Relationship between the fluorescence change of CDs vs. Sn2+ concentration (0-3.2 mM), the inset is the linear relationship between the fluorescence change of CDs vs. Sn2+ concentration (0–0.45 mM). (d) The photographs of chiral CDs in the absence and presence of Sn2+ under UV lamp and visible light. (e) TEM image of CD–Sn, the inset is size distribution of CD–Sn. (f) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CD–Sn.

(a) Fluorescence response of CD–Sn to different amino acids, F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of CD–Sn at 420 nm in the absence and presence of chiral amino acids, respectively. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CD–Sn (black solid line), CD–Sn+d-Lys (red solid line) and CD–Sn+l-Lys (blue solid line). (c) The photographs of CD–Sn, CD–Sn+d-Lys and CD–Sn+l-Lys under a 365 nm UV lamp. (d) Fluorescence spectra of CD–Sn with the addition of various concentrations ofl-Lys. (e) The linear relationships between fluorescence enhancement of CD–Sn and l-/d-Lys concentration in the range from 0 to 1.0 mM. TEM images of (f) Sn+l-Lys and (g) CD–Sn+l-Lys (Sn-l-Lys nanoclusters are marked by white dotted circles). (h) The CD spectra of CD–Sn+l-Lys and CD–Sn+d-Lys.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of L929 cells incubated with (a) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) for 1 h. (b) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and Sn2+ (0.2 mM). (c) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) and Sn2+ (0.34 mM). (d) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1), Sn2+ (0.34 mM) and l-Lys (0.6 mM). (e) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1), Sn2+ (0.34 mM) and l-Lys (2.0 mM). (f) CDs (0.1 mg mL−1), Sn2+ (0.34 mM) and d-Lys (2.0 mM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Chiral carbon dots-based nanosensors for Sn(II) detection and lysine enantiomers recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chirality has a great influence on chemical, biological, and material science. Chiral recognition is fundamental and crucial in chemistry, biology and pharmaceutics. Compared to the extensive attention on achiral carbon dots (CDs), quite a few studies on chiral CDs have been carried out, among which the effects of chiral CDs on recognizing enantiomers have rarely been explored yet. Herein, chiral CDs are utilized as a simple and efficient dual-sensor for Sn2+ detection and Lysine enantiomers recognition for the first time, with the detection limit of 0.057 μM and 3.44 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the strengths of robust stability, high sensitivity and good cytocompatibility enable CDs to successfully monitor the fluctuations of Sn2+ and l-Lys in live cells by virtue of confocal laser scanning microscope. The work highlights the potential of chiral CDs for enantiomeric recognition and ion detection.

ce:section.ce:para: All naturally occurring amino acids with the exception of glycine occur in two isomeric forms of l- and d-configurations. Only l-amino acids are an essential part of living organisms and play very important part in life activities [1]. While d-amino acids may show toxicity or metabolic abnormalities [2–4]. Therefore, the selective chiral recognition of amino acids is highly demanded especially in clinical diagnosis and therapy [5]. l-Lysine (l-Lys) is an essential amino acid, which can cross the blood-brain barrier, enter the brain tissue, provide the necessary energy source for repairing nerve cells and normal physiological activities [6]. The abnormal metabolism of l-Lys will lead to severe diseases [7]. In consideration of these serious affects, establishing efficient strategies for enantioselective recognition of Lys enantiomers is of paramount importance. Although a variety of techniques such as electrochemical methods [8], gas chromatography [9], synchrotron X-Ray powder diffraction and thermogravimetric analysis [10] have been developed for sensing Lys, most of them suffer from the drawbacks of high equipment cost, complicated sample pretreatment and time-consuming operation procedures. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a simple and effective method to detect Lys. With the unique superiorities of easy operation, fast response and high sensitivity, fluorescent spectroscopy is utilized as a more convenient technology for detecting Lys. In recent years, some fluorescent probes based on metal organic frameworks [11] and copper nanoclusters [7] have been constructed. However, the majority of sensors are subjected to tedious preparation and poor water solubility. Moreover, as far as we know, rare fluorescent probes for distinguishing l-Lys from d-Lys have been exploited. Therefore, it is still a great challenge to prepare water-soluble fluorescent sensors for chiral recognition of Lys by simple synthetic procedure.

On the other hand, as an essential trace element for humans [12], Stannous (Sn) is used worldwide in soft drinks, canned food and dentifrices, which enters mammals through the food chain [13,14]. Sn(II) ion (Sn2+) has been proved to have toxicity and genotoxicity [15–17]. Therefore, it is a critical issue to establish a reliable and sensitive method for the determination of Sn2+ in the environmental and biological systems. Although some techniques have been employed for detecting Sn2+ [15,16,18], more convenient strategies are still required to explore.

As a rising star of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been well-recognized for their remarkable advantages of unique fluorescence, facile synthesis, excellent biocompatibility and high photostability [19–30]. These advantages enable CDs to be widely used in the fields of biological imaging [19,24,31], disease diagnosis and therapy [20,22,23], and sensing [26]. As an emerging area of CDs, chiral CDs [27–29] are expected to possess great potential in chiral catalysis, chiral detection and biomedicine due to their superior steric configuration and biological activity. By now, great attention has been paid on the synthesis and applications of achiral CDs [32–36]. Nevertheless, quite a few studies on chiral CDs have been carried out. Especially, the work on chiral CDs for recognizing Lys enantiomers and Sn2+ has never been reported.

In the present work, chiral CDs were synthesized from citric acid and l-aspartic acid (l-Asp) via a facile hydrothermal method, which are able to sense Sn2+ and l-Lys by using the fluorescence switching. Sn2+ can efficiently coordinate onto the surface of CDs and form CD–Sn complex, resulting in severe fluorescence quenching (On-Off, Scheme 1 ). Because of the competition between l-Lys and CDs, when l-Lys was added to CD–Sn system, Sn2+ preferentially coordinate with l-Lys, causing the remove of Sn2+ from the surface of CDs. Thus, a significant fluorescence recovery of CDs was detected (Off-On, Scheme 1). To our surprise, no obvious luminescence restore was observed upon addition of d-Lys, which might be attributed to the steric hindrance between CD–Sn and d-Lys. Under the optimized conditions, good linearity for detecting Sn2+ was obtained over the concentration range from 0 to 0.44 mM with a detection limit of 0.057 μM, while the concentrations of l-Lys ranging from 0 to 1.0 mM exhibited a linear relationship with a detection limit of 3.34 μM. Moreover, this chiral CDs-based sensor was successfully applied for intracellular Sn2+ and l-Lys imaging, which present great potential in clinical diagnosis and drug screening.

Citric acid monohydrate (MTT), NaOH, SnCl2·2H2O were purchased from Tianjin Reagent Company. l-Asp and l-Lys were purchased from Yuanye Biological Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). d-Lys was obtained from McLean Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-Diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA). All the mentioned above chemicals do not require further purification before using.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal as described in our previous reported method [35]. Briefly, citric acid (4 mmol) was dissolved in 2 mL of water, and l-Asp (4 mmol) was fully dissolved in 6 mL of NaOH (1 M), the mixture were transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h; the crude product was cooled and dialyzed for 16 h (MWCO = 3500) to remove unreacted small molecules, and finally freeze-dried to obtain black solid sample.
The concentration of CDs is 0.1 mg mL−1 to study the stability of CDs under different conditions.

(1) Ionic strength stability. 20 μL of CDs (10 mg mL−1) were added into different concentrations of NaCl (2 mL), and the solution was uniformly mixed. Fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.
(2) Photostability. The prepared CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1, 2 mL) was irradiated with a blue LED for a period (0−60 min). Fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

(3) Thermal stability. CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1, 2 mL) was placed in a thermostatic water bath for 10 min under different temperatures. Fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.
(4) pH stability. CDs were added to a different pH buffer solution (DHP-CA buffer solution), and fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

CDs (20 μL, 10 mg mL−1) was diluted by DHP-CA buffer solution (pH = 7.4, 2.0 mL). For the detection of Sn2+, different concentrations of Sn2+ were added into the CDs solution, which were subjected to fluorescence spectrometer. For the detection of l-/d-Lys, the CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) was first mixed with Sn2+ (0.34 mM) for 0.5 h, and then various concentrations of l-/d-Lys were added for 0.5 h, after that the fluorescence spectra (λex = 340 nm) were measured. For all the samples, we selected the fluorescence intensity that corresponds to the position of its maximum emission peak for quantitative analysis.

CDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method using citric acid and l-aspartic acid (l-Asp) as precursors, which have excellent water solubility. Fig. 1 a shows the absorption spectrum of CDs. There are two peaks at 216 nm and 326 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the former is attributed to the π–π* transition of CC, and the latter is assigned to the n–π* transition of CN and CO [37]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs (Fig. 1b) indicate that CDs have excitation-dependent luminescence [38]. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is in an accordance with the double exponential decay function, which is calculated to be 9.62 ns (Table S1) [39]. The TEM (Fig. 1c) image exhibits that CDs are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 8.2 ± 1.3 nm.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analytical techniques are used to reveal elemental composition and functional groups on the surface of CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1d, the three main peaks in the XPS full spectrum of CDs are assigned to the characteristic signals of C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (399 eV), and O 1s (531 eV), respectively [37]. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 1e) can be decomposed into three peaks at 284.3 eV (CC/C=C), 285.3 eV (CN/CO), and 288.1 eV (CO). The O 1s spectra (Fig. 1f) is fitted into two peaks at 530.9 eV (CO) and 532.8 eV (CO). The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 1g) can be deconvoluted into two peaks of 399.4 eV (CN) and 400.5 eV (NH). The functional groups on the surface of CDs were further investigated by FT-IR spectra. In Fig. 1h, the bands at 3789, 3040, 1689, 1585, 1398, 1309 and 1146 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of NH, OH,C–H, CO, CC, CN and CO, respectively. The results of FT-IR are fully consistent with XPS, which verify the surface of CDs contains amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The chiroptical properties of CDs were studied by circular dichroism (CD) spectra. The CD spectrum of CDs shows two signals centered at 224 nm and 242 nm (Fig. 1i), while only one signal located at 212 nm is observed for l-Asp (Fig. S1). Thus, the signal at 224 nm is the chiral inheritance of l-Asp, the red shift at 212 nm may be due to the carbonization of pre [29]. The other CD signal at 242 nm is the new chiral center for CDs, which may be derived from the π–π* conjugation of the sp2-hybridized carbon network in CDs [28]. To better understand the crystallinity of CDs, XRD characterization was carried out. There is a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.7° (Fig. S2), corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon with lattice spacing of 0.41 nm [40], proving CDs have a highly crystalline graphite structure.

The stability of CDs was investigated in different conditions. The fluorescence intensity of CDs remained almost constant in different concentrations of NaCl solution (Fig. 2 a), verifying the favorable ionic-strength stability of CDs. The changes of fluorescence intensity were negligible even after 60 min of continuous irradiation by blue LED (Fig. 2b), indicating that CDs have high photostability. No remarkable fluorescence changes were observed at different temperatures ranging from 25 °C to 60 °C (Fig. 2c), proving that CDs have excellent thermal stability. Moreover, the luminescence difference of CDs under different pH (2.4–8.0) is not significant (Fig. 2d), demonstrating the high pH stability of CDs. Thus, pH 7.4 is chosen as the favorable pH due to its similarity to physiological environment.

The selectivity of CDs toward Sn2+ was screened by monitoring the fluorescence changes of CDs after adding different metal ions (Pb2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ag+, K+, Mg2+, Zr4+, Hf4+, Sn2+, 10 mM). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the ratio of F/F0 (where F0 and F is fluorescence intensity of CDs at 420 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively) is approximately maintained at 1.0 for Al3+, K+, Mg2+, Zr4+ and Hf4+, indicating that these ions cannot affect the luminescence of CDs. While the ions of Pb2+, Mn2+, Ag+ and Sn2+ have various degrees of quenching effects on the fluorescence of CDs, particularly, the most significant decrease of luminescence was observed in the present of Sn2+, indicating that CDs have high selectivity toward Sn2+ and can serve as a promising nanoprobe for Sn2+ detection.

Upon the addition of Sn2+ (0.34 mM) to CDs solution, the fluorescence reduced instantly and later flattened within 30 min (Fig. S3a). Therefore, 30 min of incubation is sufficient for the quenching test. Then the fluorescence titration experiments were carried out by incubating with different concentrations of Sn2+ for 30 min. As displayed in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence of CDs was gradually quenched with the increase of Sn2+ concentrations, because of the binding of Sn2+ onto the surface of CDs. Thus, CDs can sense Sn2+ by fluorescence quenching mode (On-Off). Fig. 3c indicates that the value of F 0 − F progressively increases with the enhancement of Sn2+ concentration, and then levels off when Sn2+ concentration reaches up over 1.4 mM. The illustration reveals a good linear correlation between the (F 0 − F) value (F 0 and F is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Sn2+, respectively) and Sn2+ concentration ranging from 0.0 to 0.45 mM, and the calibration curve can be fitted into F 0 − F = 25.3 + 1256.0 [Sn2+] (R 2 = 0.9937). The limit of detection (LOD) for Sn2+ is calculated to be 0.057 μM by using 3σ/κ (σ is the standard deviation and κ is the slope) [41–43]. Compared to other techniques and materials (Table S2), the CDs-based nanoprobe exhibits higher sensitivity and can provide a more convenient strategy for detecting Sn2+ in biological and environmental surveillance.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sn2+ has one peak at 216 nm (Fig. 1a). While two absorption bands (Fig. 1a) at 215 nm and 326 nm were detected in the mixture of CDs and Sn2+ (named CD–Sn), and the intensities of these two absorption bands were also enhanced, as compared with those of CDs and Sn2+, indicating the formation of CD–Sn hybrid by the coordination of Sn2+ with CO/CN groups on CDs surface [44,45].The fluorescent photographs of CDs at 365 nm in the absence and presence of Sn2+ are shown in Fig. 3d. CDs can emit bright blue color with photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QY) of CDs of 9.5%. However, the fluorescence was quenched significantly upon Sn2+ addition, and PL QY is lowered to 4.4% (Table S3), which proves that the interaction between CDs and Sn2+ causes fluorescent quenching. TEM image of CD–Sn (Fig. 3e) exhibits that CD–Sn are obviously larger than CDs with average size of 24.09 ± 5.37 nm, which can be ascribed to the complexation of Sn2+ with CDs. Furthermore, CD–Sn still maintains the same chirality as CDs. Two signals at 227 nm and 260 nm in the CD spectrum of CD–Sn (Fig. 1i) are redshifted as compared to those of CDs, which may be caused by the interaction of CDs with Sn2+. These results demonstrate that Sn2+ can coordinate with CDs to fabricate CD–Sn nanocomposites.

It is well known that fluorescence quenching can be categorized into static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) etc. [35,46,47]. We further explored the possible quenching mechanism by means of zeta potential and time-resolved fluorescence decay. The zeta potential of CDs is −1.43 mV, while zeta potential of Sn2+ is +25.8 mV. If the quenching is caused by electrostatic interaction, the zeta potential of CD–Sn should be positive. However, the zeta potential of CD–Sn is −4.96 mV, so the quenching isn’t caused by electrostatic adsorption. Meanwhile, since the emission spectrum of CDs has none overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Sn2+, ruling out the existence of FRET and IFE. The above results confirm that the coordination of Sn2+ with CDs causes fluorescence quenching. In addition, the fluorescence decay curves were conducted and displayed in Fig. 3f, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs (τCDs) and CD–Sn (τCD–Sn) is 9.62 ns and 9.32 ns (Table S1) respectively, with the τCDs/τCD–Sn ratio of 1.03 [48,49], indicating the fluorescence quenching of CDs is a static quenching accompanied by the formation of CD–Sn complex, which is consistent with the previous report [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselective optical sensing performance of CD–Sn
ce:section.ce:para: The enantioselectivity of CD–Sn toward amino acids was investigated. Various chiral amino acids (l-/d-Arg, l-/d-Asp, l-/d-Cys, l-/d-Glu, l-/d-Lys, 10 mM) were introduced into the CD–Sn complex system. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, although l-/d-Arg, l-/d-Asp, l-/d-Cys and l-/d-Glu can lead to various degrees of fluorescence recovery, there are no significant differences between l-/d isomers. By contrast, l-Lys can bring about the most obvious fluorescence enhancement, while d-Lys cannot change the luminescence of CDs. Anti-interference property of CD–Sn to l-Lys was evaluated by adding the mixture ofl-Lys and other amino acids to CD–Sn solution. The fluorescence intensities of CD–Sn+l-Lys+other amino acids were comparable to that of CD–Sn+l-Lys (Fig. 4a), proving that CD–Sn have anti-interference capability and high l-Lys selectivity.

The reason might be that l-Lys exhibits a stronger binding preference toward Sn2+ than CDs, which disturbs the interaction between CDs and Sn2+, and impel Sn2+ to remove from the surface of CDs, ultimately leading to recovery of the quenched fluorescence. Hence, CD–Sn system was explored to behave as an Off-On fluorescent probe for enantioselectively sensing l-Lys. As shown in Fig. S3b, the quenched fluorescence of CD–Sn system (0.1 mg mL−1 of CDs, 0.34 mM of Sn2+) was restored within 10 min upon the addition of l-Lys. Moreover, the fluorescence of CDs could be completely recovered by introducing l-Lys into the CD–Sn system (Fig. 4b). By contrast, CD–Sn luminescence did not change even 10 mM of d-Lys was added (Fig. 4b), and the photographs of CD–Sn, CD–Sn+d-Lys and CD–Sn+l-Lys under a 365 nm UV lamp in Fig. 4c, indicating that CD–Sn can enantioselective interact with l-Lys. Meanwhile, the PL QY of CD–Sn+l-Lys and CD–Sn+d-Lys is 8.2% and 4.7% (Table S3), respectively, further confirming that only l-Lys can induce fluorescence restore of CD–Sn. The fluorescence of CD–Sn system gradually enhanced as the concentration ofl-Lys increased (Fig. 4d). F − F 0 has a good linear relationship with lysine (0–1.0 mM), the corresponding linear regression equation is F − F 0 = 93.866 [l-Lys] − 0.205, R 2 = 0.9997 (Fig. 4e, red solid line). The limit of detection (LOD) for l-Lys was calculated to be 3.34 μM. Whereas the addition of d-Lys to CD–Sn induced no significant fluorescence change (Fig. 4e). Conclusions can be drawn from the above results that CD–Sn exhibits an enantioselective response l-Lys. Although some previous studies on probing l-Lys have been conducted (Table S4), rare work on recognizing l-/d-Lys enantiomers have been explored yet. This work provides a new approach to construct a novel enantiosensing strategy for chiral recognition of bioactive substances.

As we all know that Lys is a basic amino acid with positive charge. Amino acids have high affinity to metal ions [46,50]. The N atoms and O atoms in l-Lys may preferentially coordinate with Sn2+ to form a more stable Sn–l-Lys complex, which causes the removal of Sn2+ from the CDs surface and results in the fluorescence recovery. Nanoclusters are observed in TEM image of the mixture of Sn2+ and l-Lys (Sn+l-Lys, Fig. 4f) due to the formation of Sn–l-Lys complex. While torispherical nanoparticles and nanoclusters coexist in TEM image of the mixture of CD–Sn and l-Lys (CD–Sn+l-Lys, Fig. 4g): the former are CDs, the latter are Sn–l-Lys complexes. There were two signals at 222 nm and 258 nm in the CD spectrum of CD–Sn+l-Lys, which is basically consistent with those of CD–Sn (Fig. 4h). However, as d-Lys was introduced into CD–Sn (Fig. 4h), a new signal (210 nm) origin from d-Lys was perceived (Fig. S4), implying that d-Lys cannot coordinate with Sn2+. The results mentioned above fully prove that CD–Sn hybrid can accurately recognize the enantiomers of Lys.

Before CDs are employed for intracellular imaging applications, it is necessary to assess the cytotoxicity of CDs toward L929 cells by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S5, the cell viabilities of L929 cells are over 95% after incubating with CDs (0−500 μg mL−1) for 24 h. The result indicates that the cytotoxicity of CDs to L929 cells is negligible, and CDs have high biocompatibility. To validate the imaging application, CDs were used as the probe for confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) imaging with L929 cells. As seen in Fig. 5 a, L929 cells cultured with CDs (0.1 mg mL−1) for 1 h can emit strong green fluorescence. With the addition of different concentrations of Sn2+ (0.2 mM, 0.34 mM), the fluorescence in L929 cells gradually decreased (Fig. 5b and c). Thus, Sn2+ effectively caused intracellular fluorescence quenching. On further treatment of the L929 cells with 0.6 mM and 2.0 mM of l-Lys, respectively, and gradual fluorescence recovery was manifested (Fig. 5d and e). The results prove that CD–Sn can act as a promising probe for intracellular l-Lys detection. By contrast, d-Lys cannot restore the fluorescence of L929 cells (Fig. 5f). These results further affirm that CDs are an efficient fluorescent probe for sensing Sn2+ and recognizing the chirality of Lys in live cells.

In short, we have developed a one-step facile route to synthesize chiral CDs from citric acid and l-Asp. The fluorescence of CDs could be quenched by Sn2+ and restored by l-Lys. Based on the two processes of fluorescence On-Off and Off-On, CDs are utilized as a simple and efficient dual-sensor for the detection of Sn2+ and l-Lys with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. Due to the distinct advantages of high stability and good biocompatibility, CDs are successfully applied for intracellular Sn2+ detection and chiral recognition of Lys. It's worth mentioning that this is the first report on chiral CDs for enantioselective sensing amino acid. The work demonstrates an alternative method to construct chiral nanomaterials and highlights their great potential for enantiomeric recognition and clinical diagnosis.

Pengli Gao: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft. Zhigang Xie: Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023 and 51973214), Talent Development Fund of Jilin Province, the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200648KJ) and the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20200201088JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128265.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino acids production focusing on fermentation technologies–a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. D'Este, M. Alvarado-Morales, I. Angelidaki, Amino acids production focusing on fermentation technologies–A review. Biotechnol. Adv. 36 (2018) 14-25, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biotechadv.2017.09.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nutritional and medicinal aspects of D-amino acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Friedman, C. E. Levin, Nutritional and medicinal aspects of D-amino acids. Amino Acids 42 (2012) 1553-1582, https://doi.org/10.1007/s00726-011-0915-1.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crystal structure of human D-amino acid oxidase: context-dependent variability of the backbone conformation of the VAAGL hydrophobic stretch located at the si-face of the flavin ring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Kawazoe, H. Tsuge, M. S. Pilone, K. Fukui, Crystal structure of human D-amino acid oxidase: Context-dependent variability of the backbone conformation of the VAAGL hydrophobic stretch located at the si-face of the flavin ring. Protein Sci.15 (2006) 2708-2717, https://doi.org/10.1110/ps.062421606.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of free D-Amino acids in mammalia by chiral gas chromatography–mass spectrometry

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biosensors for determination of D and L-amino acids: a review

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengli Gao is currently a master student in the School of Chemistry and Life Sciences of Changchun University of Technology. She obtained her BS degree from Xinzhou Normal University (2017). Her research interests include the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and their applications in bioimaging and drug delivery.

Zhigang Xie is a full professor at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry (CIAC), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS). Dr Xie obtained his BS degree in Chemistry from Henan University in 2002 and his PhD degree in Polymer Chemistry from CIAC in 2007. Before joining CIAC in 2012, he worked as a postdoctoral fellow at the Polytechnic University at Brooklyn and the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. His research focuses on polymeric nanomedicines and fluorescent nanoparticles. Group Web: http://pnm.ciac.jl.cn/.

Min Zheng obtained her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2007. After working as a postdoctoral fellow at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, she joined Changchun Institute of Optics, Fine Mechanics, and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Then she moved to Changchun University of Technology as a full professor. Her research interests focus on developing fluorescent nanomaterials for biological applications.
